children.name: Petroica
children.description: The scarlet robins — a genus of small, brilliantly coloured Australasian robins with strikingly bright red or pink breast patches set against dark upperparts. They are among the most familiar and beloved birds of Australia and New Zealand, with species occurring from tropical islands to subantarctic outposts. They are upright, active insectivores that perch conspicuously and drop onto prey.

children.name: Petroica boodang
children.description: A small, striking robin with the male an unmistakable combination of glossy black upperparts, a brilliant scarlet breast, and white belly, found in southeastern and southwestern Australia. It is a territorial, upright-perching insectivore that drops from low perches onto ground-dwelling prey. It is most common in open eucalypt forests and woodlands with a grassy understorey.

children.name: Petroica phoenicea
children.description: A robin with the male a brilliant flame-orange breast and throat contrasting with grey upperparts, found in southeastern Australia. It is a partial migrant, moving from higher-elevation breeding areas to lower woodlands and farmlands in winter. Its habit of perching on fence posts and dropping onto insects makes it a conspicuous and beloved bird in rural Australia.

children.name: Petroica rodinogaster
children.description: A small, delicate robin with the male a soft rose-pink breast and throat fading to white on the belly, with dark grey upperparts, found in the cool temperate rainforests of southeastern Australia and Tasmania. It is a shy, unobtrusive bird of the damp forest understorey, often seen perched quietly on a mossy branch. Its soft, high-pitched calls are more often heard than the bird is seen.

children.name: Eopsaltria
children.description: The yellow robins — a genus of small, plump Australasian robins with bright yellow underparts and olive-grey or grey upperparts, found in forests of Australia and New Guinea. They are among the most confiding and easily observed of all Australian passerines, often perching quietly at eye level in the forest understorey. They are monogamous and maintain permanent territories in pairs.

children.name: Eopsaltria australis
children.description: A plump, friendly robin with a bright yellow breast and belly, grey-olive back, and a habit of perching quietly on low branches in the forest understorey, common along the entire eastern coast of Australia. It is well known for its trusting nature, often approaching human observers within arm's reach. It forages by pouncing on ground-dwelling prey from a low perch, returning to the same perch repeatedly.

children.name: Eopsaltria georgiana
children.description: A robin with white underparts, grey upperparts, and a pale yellow patch on the belly, restricted to the forests of southwestern Australia. It is a quiet, unobtrusive bird that perches in the lower storey of jarrah, karri, and marri forests. Like other yellow robins, it uses a perch-and-pounce foraging technique, dropping onto insects on the forest floor.

children.name: Drymodes
children.description: The scrub robins — a genus of longer-legged, more terrestrial Australasian robins with a furtive, ground-dwelling habit. They have a distinctive upright stance and a preference for dense, dry thickets and spinifex grasslands. Their longer legs allow them to run swiftly through the undergrowth, and their subtle plumage provides excellent camouflage.

children.name: Drymodes brunneopygia
children.description: A large, long-legged robin with warm brown upperparts, a pale eyebrow, and a long tail that is often cocked, found in mallee shrublands and heathy woodlands of southern Australia. It is a secretive, ground-dwelling bird that runs swiftly through low scrub and spinifex, foraging for insects. Its thin, plaintive whistle is far easier to hear than the bird itself is to observe.
